steps.title: Verify the local repository
steps.instruction: Type git status in the terminal and press Enter to verify the local repository. You can see we have some commits already in the log and we are on the main branch.

steps.title: Create an empty repository on GitHub
steps.instruction: Open your browser and go to github.com. Create a new empty repository by clicking the plus icon and selecting New repository. Give it a name such as my-local-project. Very importantly, do NOT check the Initialize this repository with a README option because we already have our local history. If you initialize it with a README there will be a conflicting history and your push will fail. Click Create repository.

steps.title: Copy the remote add command
steps.instruction: GitHub will show you a page with setup instructions since the repository is empty. Find the section that says push an existing repository from the command line. You should see two commands there. The first one is git remote add origin followed by the HTTPS or SSH URL of your repository. Copy that first command.

steps.title: Add the remote
steps.instruction: Switch back to your terminal. Paste and run that git remote add origin command. Press Enter. No output means it worked.

steps.title: Verify the remote
steps.instruction: Type git remote -v and press Enter. You should see origin listed twice, once for fetch and once for push, both pointing to your GitHub repository URL.

steps.title: Push the branch to the remote
steps.instruction: Type git push -u origin main and press Enter. The -u flag sets the upstream tracking relationship, which means in the future you can just type git push without specifying the remote and branch name. You may be prompted to enter your GitHub username and a personal access token as the password.

steps.title: Verify the push on GitHub
steps.instruction: Once authentication succeeds Git will upload your commits and you should see output showing the objects being written. Switch back to your browser and refresh the GitHub repository page. You should now see all your files and commit history there. Your local repository is now connected to GitHub and future pushes are just a git push away.
